exemplar.title: How does Corin Hardyâs use of color, time, space and sound in his film âThe Nunâ elicit fear in an audience?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does Corin Hardyâs use of color, time, space and sound in his film âThe Nunâ elicit fear in an audience?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: Topic and purpose are communicated accurately and with clear focus throughout.
Research question is clearly stated and consistently linked to the essayâs discussion.
A complete methodology is presented with appropriate range of film analyses and secondary sources.
weaknesses: Minor lapses in grammar and occasional repetition affect seamless clarity.
Covering four cinematic elements makes the focus somewhat diffuse.
Methodology lacks explicit description of close textual analysis steps.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates the topic accurately with a clear purpose and focus, and the research question is effectively integrated. Methodology is largely appropriate, drawing on a variety of sources, though source evaluation is limited and analytic steps could be more explicit. These strengths place the work solidly in the upper band, meriting 5/6.

strengths: Demonstrates solid knowledge of horror conventions and film terminology through specific scene references.
Accurate and mostly consistent use of subject-specific terms such as 'diegetic,' 'Dutch angle,' and 'desaturated palette.'
Insightful interpretation of color symbolism and strong overview of soundâs role in horror.
weaknesses: Theoretical frameworks and historical context are treated superficially.
Occasional misuse of terminology (e.g., conflating pacing with duration).
Citations often serve illustrative rather than deeply analytical purposes.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows good understanding of the topic and uses terminology appropriately for the most part. However, theoretical framing remains shallow and terminology precision occasionally lapses, placing the work in the mid-band and justifying a score of 4/6.

strengths: Presents a coherent, focused argument on how audiovisual techniques elicit fear.
Uses scene-specific analysis and well-chosen stills to support key points.
Incorporates a Harvard study on heart rate effectively to bolster evaluative claims.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on blogs, YouTube, and non-peer-reviewed sources limits academic rigour.
Some analytical claims remain asserted rather than fully evidenced (e.g., audience heart-rate fluctuations).
Minimal critique of alternative perspectives and limited discussion of methodological limitations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical thinking is solid: the student constructs a clear argument, applies research to support analysis, and integrates some evaluative evidence. However, dependence on less rigorous sources and uneven depth of evaluation constrain it to the mid performance band, meriting 6/12.

strengths: All required structural components are present and logically sequenced.
Consistent page numbering and clear section headings aid navigation.
Images are captioned and citations largely follow a consistent style.
weaknesses: Occasional malformed in-text citations and inconsistent superscript formatting.
Some image crowding and minor layout flaws impact readability.
Long paragraphs lack sentence breaks, reducing clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets requirements with a clearly appropriate structure, consistent formatting, and navigable headings. Minor citation/formating errors and layout issues prevent full marks, placing the work in the middle band and justifying a score of 3/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Primary method (close analysis of the film) is appropriate and a range of secondary sources (articles, blogs, videos, academic databases) is consulted. Yet many sources are popular/online rather than scholarly and evaluation of their credibility is limited. Selection shows some thought but not sustained critical discrimination.

Page numbers, word count and title are provided, images are captioned, and citations follow a consistent system. Minor formatting flaws (occasional malformed in-text citations, inconsistent superscript style, slight crowding of images) prevent full marks but overall requirements are met satisfactorily.

Research extends beyond the film to include interviews, health-science pieces on heart-rate, and technical blog posts, giving partial breadth. However, dependence on YouTube, blogs and non-peer-reviewed sites limits academic rigour, so relevance and quality sit at an adequate rather than robust level.

The topicâCorin Hardyâs cinematic techniques in The Nunâis introduced explicitly in the title, research question and opening paragraph. The purpose (to show how colour, time, space and sound elicit fear) is consistently referenced and the essay stays centred on those four parameters throughout. Minor lapses in clarity (some grammar issues, slight repetition) prevent a seamless read, but overall communication is accurate and purposeful.

A coherent line of argument is present (Hardy combines audiovisual tools to elicit fear) and sections conclude with mini-evaluations, but critique of alternative readings or limitations is minimal. Discussion often summarises technique rather than interrogating its effectiveness, so evaluation remains partial.

Each cinematic element is analysed with scene references and stills, and the student explains how techniques contribute to fear. Yet many claims remain asserted rather than evidenced (e.g., statements about audience heart-rate). Analytical depth is therefore intermittent, yielding partiallyâbut not fullyâsupported conclusions.

All required EE structural components are present: title page, abstract-equivalent intro, contents page, clearly headed sections, conclusion and bibliography. Logical sequencing supports reader navigation and visuals are embedded appropriately.

The student demonstrates solid knowledge of horror conventions and film terminology, and references specific scenes and production choices. Nonetheless, treatment of historical context and theoretical frameworks is superficial, and citations sometimes function as illustrative rather than analytical evidence.

Terms such as âdiegeticâ, âDutch angleâ, ânon-diegeticâ, âlow-angleâ and âdesaturated paletteâ are used largely correctly and consistently. Occasional misuse (e.g., conflating pacing with duration) and informal phrasing reduce precision, keeping usage in the âmostly accurateâ rather than âconsistently accurateâ range.

The research question is clearly stated and linked to the body of the essay; however, covering four separate cinematic elements makes the focus somewhat diffuse, leading to sections that read as descriptive catalogues rather than tightly-integrated argument. Connection is adequate but not consistently sharp.

annotations.comment: Including the word count on the title page meets formatting requirements effectively.
The introduction provides context but relies heavily on general statements; deepen theoretical framing to strengthen the analytical foundation.
The title is concise and clearly communicates the study focus on cinematographic techniques in The Nun.
The research question lists four elements (color, time, space, sound), which makes the argument diffuse. Consider narrowing to two for deeper focus.
Figure caption is descriptive but could be enhanced by explaining how the shot elicits fear.
Claim that historical setting increases realism but lacks citation; support with audience response research.
The transition between desaturated palette and natural light scenes is abrupt; add linking analysis on emotional shift.
The contrast between Sister Ireneâs white habit and her surroundings is insightful; good interpretation of color symbolism.
The critique that low-light overuse makes jumpscares predictable is perceptive; consider expanding with audience reception evidence.
The conclusion reiterates main points but offers minimal critical evaluation of limitations; integrate counter-perspectives.
Bibliography includes diverse sources but leans heavily on blogs and YouTube; prioritize scholarly articles and peer-reviewed journals.
Pages are numbered consistently; this enhances navigation and meets presentation standards.
Footnote formatting varies; adopt a consistent citation style (e.g., MLA or APA) throughout your bibliography and footnotes.
The introduction succinctly ties the context of horror conventions to your research focus, establishing clear purpose.
The statement of methodology in the introduction lacks explicit mention of close textual analysis; specify your analytic steps.
The section header âTime and Spaceâ is correctly formatted, improving navigability.
Definition of pacing conflates pacing with duration; refine terminology and cite a film theory source for precision.
Sentence breaks are needed for readability; some paragraphs extend excessively without sub-structure.
Sound section opens with a strong overview of auditory importance in horror, showing solid understanding of concept.
Discussion of non-diegetic sound is interrupted; complete the explanation to support your evaluative claims.
The detailed use of Harvard study strengthens your argument on heart-rate impact; well applied research.
Reliance on broadcast media for heart-rate data limits academic rigour; supplement with peer-reviewed research.
Color and light section articulates visual impact clearly, demonstrating coherent understanding of film language.
The RPPF first reflection is descriptive; add analytical reflection on how initial planning influenced methodology choices.
Interim reflection highlights adaptability but could critically evaluate how feedback shaped analysis depth.
Final reflection shows personal growth but would benefit from specific examples of methodological adjustments.
Supervisor initials are missing on RPPF reflections; ensure all sessions are authenticated.
The supervisor comment section remains blank; include supervisor feedback to validate authenticity.
